exercises.title: Using the math Module
exercises.description: Python's math module provides mathematical functions. Import it and use several of its functions.
exercises.starterCode:
import math

# TODO: Print the value of math.pi
print(math.pi)

# TODO: Print the square root of 144
print(math.sqrt(144))

# TODO: Print floor and ceil of 3.7
print(math.floor(3.7))
print(math.ceil(3.7))

# TODO: Print log base 10 of 1000
print(math.log10(1000))
hints: import math makes all math module functions available as math.function().
math.sqrt() returns a float; math.floor() and math.ceil() return integers.

exercises.title: Selective Imports and Aliases
exercises.description: Import specific names from a module, or import a module with an alias.
exercises.starterCode:
# Import specific functions
from math import sqrt, factorial, gcd

print(sqrt(225))
print(factorial(6))
print(gcd(48, 18))

# Import with alias
import math as m

print(m.pow(2, 10))
print(round(m.e, 4))
hints: 'from math import sqrt' lets you call sqrt() directly without 'math.'.
'import math as m' lets you use m.function() as a shorter alias.

exercises.title: Using the random Module (Deterministic Seed)
exercises.description: The random module generates random numbers. By seeding the random number generator, we get deterministic (reproducible) output.
exercises.starterCode:
import random

# Seed for deterministic output
random.seed(42)

# TODO: Print 5 random integers between 1 and 10
for _ in range(5):
    print(random.randint(1, 10))

print()

# TODO: Pick a random item from a list
random.seed(7)
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "mango", "grape"]
print(random.choice(fruits))
hints: random.seed(42) makes the random sequence deterministic and reproducible.
random.randint(1, 10) returns a random integer from 1 to 10 inclusive.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: import Syntax
warmup.description: Modules extend Python with additional functions. Three import styles.
